f196e53c1c962dc9.summary: An ecosystem includes all biotic (plants, animals, microbes) and abiotic (sunlight, temperature, soil, water) components interacting in a habitat. Biotic components are producers, consumers, and decomposers.

chunks.title: Understanding Ecosystems
chunks.content:
**Ecosystem and its Components** is a fundamental concept in Our Environment for CBSE Class X Science.

**Ecosystem:** All interacting organisms (biotic) and non-living (abiotic) factors in a defined area.

**Abiotic Components:**
• Sunlight — primary energy source
• Temperature — affects metabolism
• Water — essential for life
• Soil — provides nutrients and anchorage
• Air — CO₂, O₂ for photosynthesis/respiration

**Biotic Components:**
• Producers (Autotrophs): Green plants, algae — make food via photosynthesis
• Consumers (Heterotrophs): Animals that eat plants/other animals
• Decomposers (Saprotrophs): Bacteria, fungi — break down dead matter

chunks.title: Producers and Consumers
chunks.content:
**Producers (Autotrophs):**
• Convert solar energy into chemical energy (photosynthesis)
• 6CO₂ + 12H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ + 6H₂O
• Form the first trophic level
• E.g., Green plants, phytoplankton, algae

**Consumers (Heterotrophs):**
• **Primary consumers (Herbivores):** Eat producers (cow, deer, rabbit)
• **Secondary consumers:** Eat herbivores (snake, frog, cat)
• **Tertiary consumers:** Eat secondary consumers (eagle, lion, shark)
• **Omnivores:** Eat both plants and animals (human, bear, crow)

**Decomposers:**
• Break down dead organisms into simpler substances
• Return nutrients to soil → used again by producers
• E.g., Bacteria, fungi (mushrooms, mould)

chunks.title: Trophic Levels and Energy Flow
chunks.content:
**Trophic Levels:** The position an organism occupies in a food chain.

**10% Law (Lindemann's Law):** Only 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next. The rest is:
• Used in respiration
• Lost as heat
• Used for growth, reproduction, excretion

**Energy Flow is Unidirectional:**
Sun → Producer → Primary Consumer → Secondary Consumer → Tertiary Consumer

**Ecological Pyramids:**
• **Pyramid of Numbers:** Number of organisms at each level
• **Pyramid of Biomass:** Biomass at each level
• **Pyramid of Energy:** Always upright — energy decreases at each level

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** In a grassland ecosystem, sunflowers (1000 kcal) are eaten by rabbits, who are eaten by snakes, who are eaten by eagles. Calculate energy available at each trophic level.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Producers (sunflowers) = 1000 kcal
**Step 2:** Primary consumers (rabbits) = 10% of 1000 = 100 kcal
**Step 3:** Secondary consumers (snakes) = 10% of 100 = 10 kcal
**Step 4:** Tertiary consumers (eagles) = 10% of 10 = 1 kcal

**Answer:** 1000 kcal → 100 kcal → 10 kcal → 1 kcal

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Identify the biotic and abiotic components in a pond ecosystem.

**Answer:** Biotic — fish, algae, frogs, bacteria. Abiotic — water, sunlight, temperature, dissolved O₂, pH.

---

**Q2.** Why are decomposers essential for an ecosystem?

**Answer:** They decompose dead matter, recycle nutrients back to soil, which producers use. Without decomposers, nutrients would be locked in dead remains.

---

**Q3.** What happens to the energy that is not transferred to the next trophic level?

**Answer:** Used in respiration, growth, reproduction; lost as heat to the environment.

02013f649f24bf49.summary: Food chain: linear sequence of who eats whom (grass→deer→lion). Food web: interconnected food chains. Biological magnification: non-biodegradable chemicals (DDT) accumulate at higher trophic levels.

chunks.title: Understanding Food Chains
chunks.content:
**Food Chains and Webs** is a fundamental concept in Our Environment for CBSE Class X Science.

**Food Chain:** A linear sequence showing who eats whom, depicting energy transfer from one organism to another.

**Example:**
Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Eagle

**Types:**
1. **Grazing Food Chain:** Starts with producers (plants)
   • Terrestrial: Grass → Deer → Lion
   • Aquatic: Phytoplankton → Zooplankton → Small fish → Large fish
2. **Detritus Food Chain:** Starts with dead organic matter
   • Dead leaves → Earthworm → Bird

chunks.title: Food Webs
chunks.content:
**Food Web:** An interconnecting network of multiple food chains in an ecosystem.

**Why food webs are important:**
• More stable than single food chains
• If one species declines, organisms can eat alternative food
• Shows complex feeding relationships

**Example of a forest food web:**
• Oak tree → Caterpillar → Bird → Hawk
• Oak tree → Deer → Wolf
• Oak tree → Squirrel → Fox → Wolf
• Berries → Mouse → Owl

All these chains interconnect — a single species (e.g., oak tree) supports multiple consumers.

chunks.title: Biological Magnification and Ozone Depletion
chunks.content:
**Biological Magnification (Biomagnification):**
Increase in concentration of a non-biodegradable substance (pesticide, heavy metal) at each successive trophic level.

**Example — DDT:**
• Water: 0.003 ppb → Phytoplankton: 0.04 ppm (×10⁴) → Zooplankton: 0.5 ppm → Small fish: 2 ppm → Large fish: 25 ppm → Fish-eating birds: 5-100 ppm
• Result: Bird eggshells thin → population decline

**Ozone Depletion:**
• O₂ + UV → O₃ (ozone in stratosphere)
• O₃ absorbs harmful UV-B radiation
• CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) from refrigerators, ACs destroy O₃
• CFCs → Cl + O₃ → ClO + O₂ (catalytic destruction)
• 1987 Montreal Protocol banned CFCs globally

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Construct a food chain from: grass, frog, eagle, grasshopper, snake. Which organism has the highest concentration of non-biodegradable pesticides?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Arrange by feeding: Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Eagle
**Step 2:** Through biomagnification, DDT concentration increases at each trophic level
**Step 3:** Eagle is at the highest trophic level

**Answer:** Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Eagle. The eagle will have the highest concentration of pesticides.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Why are food webs more stable than food chains?

**Answer:** Multiple feeding options exist — if one species declines, organisms can switch to alternative food sources.

---

**Q2.** If pesticide enters a pond ecosystem, which organism will have the maximum concentration?

**Answer:** The top predator (e.g., large fish or fish-eating bird) due to biological magnification.

---

**Q3.** What is the role of CFCs in ozone depletion?

**Answer:** CFCs release chlorine atoms in the stratosphere, which catalytically destroy ozone molecules (Cl + O₃ → ClO + O₂).

ccbc4e54a9393cbf.summary: Human activities like waste generation, deforestation, and pollution harm the environment. Non-biodegradable waste persists. Proper waste management (reduce, reuse, recycle, compost, landfill) is essential for sustainability.

chunks.title: Impact of Human Activities on Environment
chunks.content:
**How do our Activities affect the Environment?** is a fundamental concept in Our Environment for CBSE Class X Science.

Human activities significantly alter ecosystems:

**1. Waste Generation:**
• **Biodegradable waste:** Can be decomposed by microorganisms (fruit peels, paper, wood, cotton)
• **Non-biodegradable waste:** Cannot be decomposed (plastics, glass, metal, DDT, synthetic fibres)

**2. Deforestation:**
• Reduces CO₂ absorption → increased greenhouse effect
• Soil erosion, loss of biodiversity, climate change

**3. Pollution:**
• Air: Burning fossil fuels, industrial emissions
• Water: Industrial effluents, sewage, agricultural runoff
• Soil: Pesticides, chemical fertilisers, plastic waste

chunks.title: Waste Management
chunks.content:
**Waste Management Strategies:**

**3Rs — Reduce, Reuse, Recycle:**
• **Reduce:** Minimise waste generation (carry cloth bags, avoid single-use plastics)
• **Reuse:** Use items repeatedly (refillable bottles, repaired electronics)
• **Recycle:** Convert waste into new products (paper, glass, metal, plastic)

**Composting:**
• Biodegradable waste → compost (natural fertiliser)
• Aerobic decomposition by microorganisms
• Reduces landfill burden

**Landfills:**
• Dumping waste in low-lying areas
• Non-biodegradable waste ends up here
• Problems: Leachate pollutes groundwater, methane gas, space shortage

**Vermicomposting:** Using earthworms to decompose organic waste

chunks.title: Global Environmental Issues
chunks.content:
**Greenhouse Effect and Global Warming:**
• CO₂, CH₄, H₂O vapour trap heat in atmosphere
• Enhanced by burning fossil fuels, deforestation
• Consequences: Melting glaciers, rising sea levels, extreme weather

**Ozone Layer Depletion:**
• Ozone (O₃) in stratosphere absorbs UV-B radiation
• CFCs, halons, methyl bromide destroy ozone
• Effects: Skin cancer, cataracts, reduced crop yield

**Sustainable Practices:**
• Use renewable energy (solar, wind, hydro)
• Plant trees (afforestation)
• Segregate waste at source (wet/dry)
• Say NO to single-use plastics
• Carpooling, public transport

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Classify as biodegradable or non-biodegradable: plastic bag, vegetable peels, glass bottle, paper, DDT, cotton cloth.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Biodegradable — broken down by microorganisms: vegetable peels, paper, cotton cloth
**Step 2:** Non-biodegradable — cannot be broken down: plastic bag, glass bottle, DDT

**Answer:** Biodegradable: vegetable peels, paper, cotton cloth. Non-biodegradable: plastic bag, glass bottle, DDT.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the difference between biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste? Give two examples of each.

**Answer:** Biodegradable (decomposed by microbes): fruit peels, wood. Non-biodegradable (cannot decompose): plastic, glass.

---

**Q2.** Why should we segregate waste into wet and dry categories?

**Answer:** Wet waste can be composted (biodegradable). Dry waste can be recycled. Mixing makes both processes difficult.

---

**Q3.** Suggest three ways to reduce plastic pollution.

**Answer:** 1. Use cloth/jute bags. 2. Carry reusable water bottles. 3. Refuse single-use plastic straws and cutlery.
